# Transcrição: 04-Email Marketing Flowcharts

- **Fonte:** 04-Email Marketing Flowcharts

- **Idioma:** en

## [00:00 - 03:01]

All right, so we're going to go over quickly the email marketing flow charts. The first one is all about email list segmentation. And keep in mind that these are hypothetical examples. You can set up your business any way that you want. We just wanted to give you something to work from to help you really visualize how this could work. So, these yellow boxes represent different lists that you could have inside of your email creator. Now, the important thing to remember is that you can have as many lists as you'd like. And also, that subscribers can be on more than one list at any given time. Now, you can have people go on one list and come off of another. That's very, very easy to do. But just keep in mind you can have as many of these lists or think of them as buckets as you'd like to have. So, in this example, we've got a general subscriber list. As you'll notice, you want to have all new subscribers always go on to this list. This might be the default list or whatever you want to call it. So, what you'll see, all these ovals here represent different ways that people could get on to your list. So, if somebody fills out a web form, always have them go into your general subscriber list. If they download a report, go to your list. There could be a hundred different ways how people could get on to this list, but just want to give you a good idea. The next list that we've created here in our hypothetical example is the sales follow-up list. So, this is a list you might use for various reasons. So, say for example, you've got a product in an email and somebody clicks on a link. You could add them to a sales follow-up list like, hey, this person has interest in this, send them additional emails. Or, for example, let's say you wanted to make a shopping cart abandon our sequence. So, you've got your shopping cart broken into two separate steps. If somebody completes the first step, boom, add them to a sales follow-up list. You can have more than one sales follow-up list. You could have one for product A, product B. However you want to do it, just keep in mind this is your business. You set it up however you see fit. The next one is a paid customer list. So, if somebody purchases online, add them to this list. If they purchase on the phone, add them to this list. Next one is a VIP subscriber list. In this hypothetical example, we just wanted to make a list that's really going to tell us, hey, these are the most important people. They either bought a high ticket item or maybe they signed up as an affiliate, somebody who wants to promote your products and services. So just really keep it very simple at first. As you go, you will get more complex and you'll get more segmented. But right now, what really matters is you've got just a good general idea of how you want to segment it and how people are going to flow into your various lists. Also at the bottom here, we've given you some examples of exclusions. If you want to send out emails, for example, and let's just say you've got somebody gets added to a paid customer list, you can have them removed from the sales follow-up list.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

So, let's just say somebody bans a cart and then boom, they buy the product, you might have them taken off of this follow-up list right here. Then the added to a VIP subscriber list. Maybe we want, if they're on this list, we don't want them to be on this list. Now we could keep them on both if we wanted to, but these are just examples of how you might want to do it. If somebody gets added to your sales follow-up list, then maybe what you do is you temporarily stop sending them general broadcasts. So let's just say that you've got a high ticket item, somebody fills out an application, they're now getting follow-up to buy your high ticket product, and let's just say you're promoting a low dollar product for $19, well, you might want to stop sending them the $19 messages if they're showing an interest in buying a high ticket item. So those are just examples of ways that you might want to exclude people. The good news is email marketers now make this relatively simple to do, and any way that you want to segment your list, you're able to do so. So that's the first flow chart. Let's go to flow chart number two. This one is a little bit more involved. This is the customer email journey. This is the potential path that a lead might take through your auto responder. Your business is clearly going to be different. But if you look at this, we've set this up so it includes some of the major sequences that we teach you about. So this is an indoctrination sequence. So if we look at this, all of these sequences have a trigger, something that starts it, and then a goal, something that ends it. Now keep this in mind, sometimes the goal is just that the sequence ends, or sometimes it's that the user has taken a specific action. So we just wanted to show you the path that a user could take. For you, you're going to just take out a good old fashioned piece of paper and think about this in your head, what does this journey look like for my particular business? So in this, we're just going to walk through this step by step. A new lead comes in. They go through your indoctrination sequence, which you've already learned is basically your way of warming them up and building a relationship. So they get an email, there's a one day delay, they get another email, a two day delay, and then another email, and then the goal is they reach the end of the sequence. Then in this particular journey, we just follow the arrow, there's a trigger of they finish the sequence, and then what it does is it goes into a sales sequence. So we warmed them up. Cool. Now let's go in for the kill. So we've got a one day delay, an email, a one day delay, an email, two day delay. You can make these delays any amount that you want. As you know, Grant likes to send email, so he's not shy with it. But if you want to be somebody who waits a couple days or whatever that might be, you

## [06:02 - 07:53]

can feel free. So we've got a sales sequence. You might want to make it longer than three days. We just put three days because it fit on a piece of paper, but I would recommend sending more than three sales emails. So as this sequence goes, there's a couple ways that it could end. Number one is they could abandon cart. And if you want to take them out once they abandon cart, you could keep it going. Something says you have to actually stop the sequence when they abandon cart. But in this example, they abandon cart. We're going to trigger an abandon cart sequence to start, and then abandon cart emails go out. And that continues until they buy the product. So if you see here, we've actually got a couple different ways. So this right here, boom, they're going through the sales sequence. And if they buy the product, what happens? It triggers the byproduct, the buyer sequence. So this is where we're going to say, hey, thank you so much for being on board. Here's access to whatever. Our team is here to help. But also, if we've got a higher end product, we could upsell them as well. So you'll also see that somebody could come from the abandon cart sequence and trigger the sequence to happen as well. So this is, again, just an illustration to show you what is possible and give you some ideas. But in your world, what really matters is start by building each of these individual sequences and then try to start tying them together so that you can make a customer journey. And while this does look a little bit daunting at first, keep in mind that once this thing is working, it will keep working day in and day out. It will be your sales team, your support team, 24 hours a day, seven days a week. And that is the power of automated email. Go get them.
